summary: Gruber dissects three distinct 'Apple bear' arguments about why Apple is supposedly doomed in the mobile market. He draws on Ben Thompson's critique of Clayton Christensen's disruption theory to argue that design quality creates a sustainable advantage in consumer markets that can never be 'overshot.' Using the Mac's own history, he demonstrates that Apple's darkest period correlated with loss of design leadership, not low market share, and that the Mac-vs-Windows comparison actually supports Apple's current strategy. He concludes that Apple need not win majority market share to thrive — it just needs to keep delighting customers at the high end.
key_points: Consumer markets differ fundamentally from business markets: ease-of-use and design quality are attributes that can never be overshot, making disruption theory less applicable
The Mac's near-death experience in the 1990s was caused by loss of design and engineering leadership, not by low market share — market share was the effect, not the cause
The 'network effects' argument (iOS is the new Mac, Android is the new Windows) is undermined by the internet being a universal platform that eliminates the compatibility lock-in of the 1990s
Apple's disproportionate profit share reflects a demographically distinct customer base willing to pay for superior design, not random market distribution
The three Apple bear arguments are largely contradictory and, ironically, the Mac's survival in the PC market refutes arguments 1 and 3 simultaneously
money_quotes: Low market share was the effect of the Mac's problems, not the cause.

The single decision that came closest to bankrupting Apple was the one to license Mac OS to hardware cloners — a decision that was all about attempting to increase market share for the sake of increasing market share.
Today, compatibility is a rarely uttered word.
Apple's 10 percent of the market is entirely comprised of the high end. Mac users are discriminating, willing to pay more for a product they deem superior.
This has nothing to do with focusing on raw market share, and everything to do with keeping the pedal to the metal on design and quality.
"Some" need not be "most", or even all that close to "most", for Apple to maintain a lucrative position, with a minority of total unit share but a majority of the profits.
The only catch: they need to keep delighting customers.
key_insight: Design quality and customer delight create a sustainable competitive advantage in consumer markets that cannot be overshot or disrupted by 'good enough' low-end competitors, as the Mac's own history demonstrates.
